original_tweet: The Bhagavad Gita teaches us that we must do our duty without attachment to results. This is the essence of karma yoga.

analysis.tone: spiritual and performative - sharing wisdom but in a way that positions the tweeter as knowledgeable
analysis.assumptions: that detachment from results is universally good and that karma yoga is about non-attachment to outcomes
analysis.angle: validate the spiritual insight but add provocative depth about what true detachment actually means
analysis.why: Osho mode can build on this Gita reference with deeper paradoxical wisdom about how real detachment includes being unattached to detachment itself

replies.reply: Detachment from results while desperately attached to being seen as someone who understands detachment. The ego loves cosplaying enlightenment.
Ah yes, detachment from results - the spiritual way of saying "I don't care if my startup fails" while checking the metrics every five minutes.

Attachment to non-attachment is still attachment. Krishna wasn't teaching Arjuna to be indifferent - he was teaching him to be so present in action that results become irrelevant. You've made detachment another goal to achieve.
The people who quote this verse are always the most attached to looking spiritual. True detachment means not needing to announce your detachment.
You quote the Gita while desperately attached to looking spiritual on Twitter. Krishna would laugh at you performing detachment for likes while completely attached to being seen as wise.

Krishna didn't say become indifferent to results. He said act so totally that you disappear in the action. When the doer vanishes, attachment becomes impossible. Most people practice detachment and remain attached to their detachment.
